topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Dr. Albright was leaving. My thesis advisor. The words swam before my eyes, blurring the screen until all I could see was the stark, black text announcing his departure date. A slow, hot wave of anger started in my chest, tightening my throat until I could barely draw a breath. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet dorm room. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I stood up, pacing the cramped space, the floorboards protesting beneath my agitated steps. Every meticulously crafted sentence, every late night spent wrestling with my data, felt suddenly invalidated, rendered meaningless by this administrative decree. I needed to scream, to tear the very air apart, but the sound caught in my throat, replaced by a low, vibrating hum of pure resentment.

[story 2]

"He's gone," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I stared at the framed photograph on my desk—Dr. Albright smiling, looking impossibly composed. The picture seemed to mock me. My jaw was locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I walked to the window, the city lights sprawling below like indifferent constellations. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped me, quickly followed by a tightening in my shoulders. I wanted to smash something, anything, to release the pressure building behind my ribs. I turned back to my desk, my gaze sweeping over the stacks of annotated papers. Each page represented a commitment, a shared journey, and now, it felt like wreckage. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling cold and alien in my hand, and began to savagely cross out lines of text, the ink bleeding slightly where my grip was too forceful.

[story 3]

I reread the announcement three times, each time the feeling escalating into a tight, hot knot just beneath my sternum. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of my desk, the wood groaning under the pressure. I didn't move for a long time, just stood there, breathing in shallow, rapid gasps. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, stripped of all warmth. "This is ridiculous," I managed, the inflection sharp enough to cut glass. I walked over to the whiteboard, my shadow stretching long and distorted under the fluorescent lights. I didn't write; instead, I grabbed a dry-erase marker and began aggressively scribbling large, chaotic symbols across the surface, a visual representation of the internal turmoil churning within me. The frustration manifested as a desperate need for control, a frantic attempt to impose order on the swirling chaos of betrayal and sudden abandonment.
